title: Play-to-Earn in the UK 2026: Games, Real Earnings & HMRC Tax Explained
html:
Play to earn in the UK is legal in 2026: there is no law against earning crypto tokens or NFTs from games, and British players do it openly. The catch is twofold. First, crypto rewards sit outside the FCA's consumer-protection perimeter, so the tokens you earn are high-risk and largely unregulated. Second, HMRC generally treats what you earn as taxable, usually as income when it lands in your wallet and again as a capital gain when you later sell it. This guide covers the legal position, the tax treatment in plain terms, and which games actually pay UK players, including DeHub's own arcade.

None of the below is financial or tax advice. Crypto's value can fall as well as rise, you may not get your money back, and your personal position depends on your circumstances. For anything that touches your return, check the current guidance on GOV.UK or speak to a qualified accountant.

Is play-to-earn legal in the UK?

Yes. Playing a game that rewards you in cryptocurrency or NFTs is entirely legal for UK residents. What is regulated is not the playing but the assets themselves and how they are marketed. Since October 2023 the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has required crypto promotions aimed at UK consumers to carry clear risk warnings and a cooling-off period for first-time buyers, and that regime has only tightened since.

The practical implication for play to earn games UK players should hold onto: the token you win is a high-risk, unregulated asset. If the game folds, the token collapses, or you lose your keys, there is no Financial Services Compensation Scheme safety net and no ombudsman to appeal to. Legal does not mean protected.

FCA risk warning. Don't invest unless you're prepared to lose all the money you invest. Crypto is a high-risk investment and you are unlikely to be protected if something goes wrong. Take two minutes to learn more before you buy or play.

A few games also blur into gambling if rewards depend on chance and a stake. Genuine play-to-earn, where payouts follow skill, time, or achievement rather than a wager, generally sits outside the Gambling Commission's remit, but loot-box-style mechanics remain a grey area worth avoiding if you want a clean, above-board setup.

How does HMRC tax play-to-earn? (the general rule)

Here is the part most UK players get wrong. HMRC does not have a special "gaming" tax, so crypto gaming rewards fall under existing rules for crypto assets. In general terms there are two separate taxable moments.

1. Income tax when you receive the reward

When tokens or NFTs arrive in your wallet as a reward for playing, watching, or completing tasks, HMRC generally treats them as miscellaneous income, valued in pounds sterling at the market value on the day you received them. That sterling figure is added to your other income and taxed at your marginal rate, broadly 20%, 40%, or 45% depending on your band. This is the core of play to earn tax UK questions, and it applies whether or not you ever convert the tokens to cash.

2. Capital gains tax when you dispose of it

Later, when you sell those tokens, swap them for another crypto, or spend them, that is a disposal for capital gains purposes. Your gain is the sale value minus the value you were already taxed on as income (your cost basis). Gains above the annual CGT allowance are taxed at the prevailing CGT rates. Note the allowance has been cut sharply in recent years, so more small players now cross the threshold than did a few seasons ago.

Two extra points on crypto gaming tax HMRC watchers should note for 2026:

  • Records are on you. You must value each reward in sterling at the time of receipt and keep a log of dates, amounts, and rates. Reconstructing a year of small rewards after the fact is painful, so track as you go.
  • Reporting is getting automatic. From January 2026 the UK adopts the international Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF), under which exchanges and providers report user data to tax authorities. The old assumption that small crypto activity stays invisible no longer holds.

You declare crypto through Self Assessment, with the online deadline on 31 January following the tax year. Again, this is general information, not advice for your situation, so confirm the current thresholds and rates on GOV.UK or with an accountant before you file.

For the token side of the equation, our DeHub crypto buying guide walks through acquiring and holding $DHB from the UK, which pairs naturally with tracking what your play earns.

Play-to-earn games UK players can actually use in 2026

Plenty of "earn" games are geo-blocked, pay in dead tokens, or demand a costly NFT before you can start. The genuinely UK-relevant ones share three traits: no forced upfront spend, a token you can actually trade, and a game that is fun to play whether or not the token moons.

Last Chad Standing

DeHub's flagship game, Last Chad Standing, is an MMA fighter battle royale built around licensed fighters. It runs inside the wider DeHub, the decentralised social network rather than as a standalone app, so your progress and rewards live alongside your feed, your uploads, and your wallet. Because it sits on the same on-chain rails as the rest of the platform, rewards settle in $DHB rather than in a throwaway in-game currency you can never cash out.

The DeHub play-to-earn arcade

Around Last Chad Standing sits a broader play-to-earn arcade, a set of lighter games that reward time and skill without an entry fee. This is the low-friction on-ramp: you can start earning without buying anything, which keeps you clear of the "pay to play to earn" trap that sinks so many projects. If you want a wider survey of no-cost options, our roundup of free play-to-earn games with no investment covers the field.

Watch-to-earn and the wider "X-to-earn" family

Play-to-earn is one branch of a bigger tree. On DeHub, watch-to-earn bounties reward you for watching content, and the model extends into move-to-earn elsewhere. If you are weighing which fits your habits, our comparison of watch-to-earn, play-to-earn and move-to-earn breaks down the effort-to-reward trade-off of each. For most UK players, mixing a bit of watch to earn UK activity with arcade play spreads the effort out sensibly.

Whichever branch you pick, the tax logic above is identical: value the reward in sterling on receipt, then account for any gain on disposal.

How to get started with play-to-earn in the UK

A clean, above-board setup takes about an evening. Here is the practical sequence.

  • Set up a wallet. A self-custody wallet on Base (an Ethereum Layer 2) gives you control of your keys and low fees. Write your recovery phrase down offline and never share it. On DeHub, your content and rewards are minted on-chain the moment you act, so the wallet is your hub.
  • Start with no-spend games first. Prove the mechanics pay out before you commit a penny. The arcade and watch-to-earn bounties let you test the full earn-and-withdraw loop with zero risk.
  • Understand the token before you hold it. If you decide to acquire $DHB rather than only earn it, read up first. $DHB trades on DEXs and on Coinbase and is listed on CoinGecko and CoinMarketCap. Never treat a game token as a savings account.
  • Log every reward as you earn it. Date, token, quantity, and the sterling value that day. This one habit turns January's Self Assessment from a nightmare into a copy-and-paste job.
  • Keep the FCA warning front of mind. Only ever risk what you can afford to lose entirely.

When you are ready, you can jump straight into the games and the feed at the DeHub web app, or grab it from Google Play. Everything is open-source and user-owned, so you are not renting access to your own rewards.

Why DeHub is a sensible UK starting point

Two things make DeHub a practical home base for a British player. First, it is built by a UK company: DeHub is run by DeLabs Ltd, a United Kingdom company, whose co-founders Mike Hales and Indi Jay Cammish also founded First Class Agency, the UK's number-one official TikTok LIVE partner agency. That is a team that understands the UK creator economy from the inside, and the project sponsors the North Wales Crusaders rugby league club.

Second, play-to-earn here is not bolted onto a shell. It sits inside a full platform: a chronological, transparent feed with no opaque algorithm, tokenised uploads, on-chain tradable subscriptions with resale royalties, up to 90% ad-revenue share to the ecosystem, and live streaming on Livepeer that has scaled to 50,000-plus concurrent viewers. The games are one way to earn among several, which means your effort is not hostage to a single token's fortunes.

One last disambiguation, because search engines conflate them: the DeHub covered here is the Web3 social network at dehub.io. It is unrelated to DePaul University's "DeHUB" student portal or Rowan University's "DEHub" digital engineering hub. If you landed here looking for a campus login, this is not it.

Frequently asked questions

Is play-to-earn legal in the UK?

Yes. Earning crypto or NFTs from games is legal for UK residents in 2026. However, the tokens are high-risk and fall outside the FCA's consumer-protection rules, so you have no compensation scheme if things go wrong. Games with wager-and-chance mechanics can also stray into gambling territory, which is separately regulated.

Do I pay tax on play-to-earn earnings?

Generally, yes. HMRC usually treats rewards as taxable income at their sterling value when you receive them, and then as a capital gain when you later sell or swap them. This applies even if you never convert to cash. Thresholds and rates change, so check GOV.UK or an accountant; this is general information, not tax advice.

How does HMRC treat crypto gaming rewards?

There is no dedicated gaming tax. HMRC applies its existing crypto-asset rules: income tax on receipt (typically as miscellaneous income) and capital gains tax on disposal, with the gain measured against the value you were already taxed on. You report through Self Assessment by 31 January, and from 2026 providers report data to HMRC under the CARF framework.

What is the best play-to-earn game for UK players?

The best fit is one with no forced upfront spend and a genuinely tradable token. DeHub's Last Chad Standing MMA battle royale and its play-to-earn arcade meet both tests and, being built by a UK company, keep rewards on-chain in $DHB rather than in a locked in-game currency. Start with the no-cost options and prove the payout loop before spending anything.

Can I play-to-earn without spending any money?

Yes. Free-to-start games avoid the "pay to play to earn" trap entirely. DeHub's arcade and watch-to-earn bounties let you test the full earn-and-withdraw cycle at zero risk, and our guide to free play-to-earn games with no investment lists more options for UK players.
